grants.name: Energy Efficiency and Conservation Block Grant (EECBG)
grants.purpose: Funds a comprehensive fleet efficiency study and transition plan (began 2025); a water and energy conservation outreach program (1,000+ free water/energy conservation kits via GSO WaterWise); a study to identify buildings to upgrade to net-zero energy and purchase equipment for ongoing energy management; and a comprehensive greenhouse gas inventory.
grants.statusNote: 721 residents registered for a water/energy kit as of December 2025. Fleet telematics pilot (100 vehicles) began July 2025.

fundedActivities.activity: Comprehensive fleet efficiency study and transition plan
Long-term planning, building upgrades, monitoring/analytics, renewable energy exploration, educational content
Vehicle inventory / equipment purchase / lease process analysis (folded into the fleet efficiency study)
Water and energy conservation kits + outreach in low-wealth areas; net-zero building candidate study; comprehensive GHG inventory prep
GSO WaterWise water/energy conservation kit distribution

grants.fundedActivitiesNote: Confirmed across the 2024 and 2025 Progress Reports as funding five distinct activities spanning three recommendations and two department goals from a single $314,150 award — reflected here as a list rather than split into per-activity dollar amounts, since no source document allocates specific dollars to each activity.

grants.name: Duke Energy Foundation Grant
grants.purpose: $10,000 allocated to the Community Safety Department for essential supplies during extreme temperatures; $15,000 for tree purchases (remaining funds supporting tree-planting efforts).
grants.verificationNote: This grant's amount/date/purpose are stated directly in the 2025 Progress Report (Tier 2 citation), which is sufficient sourcing on its own. An attempt to independently corroborate via a specific Legistar file number ('2025-705') did not turn up a confirmable record in this research pass — no Legistar/Tier 3 citation is added; the entry correctly remains sourced to progress2025 alone.

grants.name: National Environmental Education Foundation (NEEF) Greening STEM Grant
grants.purpose: Environmental education programming for middle school students using STEM-based instruction; also supports tree planting and educational programming with local schools. 16 middle school students registered as of December 2025.
grants.verificationNote: Amount/date/purpose are stated directly in the 2025 Progress Report (Tier 2 citation), sufficient sourcing on its own. NEEF's own public grants page independently corroborates the $20,000 figure (2 grants of $10,000 issued to Greensboro's PPG-cohort Greening STEM program, spring 2025), which is a helpful cross-check even though it's not a formal citation here. An attempt to independently corroborate via a specific Legistar file number ('2025-706') did not turn up a confirmable record in this research pass — no Legistar/Tier 3 citation is added.

grants.name: USDA Forest Service Tree Canopy Grant
grants.source: US Department of Agriculture Forest Service (Inflation Reduction Act funding)
grants.purpose: 5-year project to conduct an updated tree canopy study and management plan, and engage disadvantaged communities of Greensboro. One of 842 proposals reviewed nationally (of $6.4 billion in requests); overseen by OSR with support from other City departments. This is the grant behind the 'Keeping the Green in Greensboro: Tree Canopy and Equity Prioritization Study' (kicked off Sept. 2024, assessment completed Dec. 2024, Equity Prioritization Tool completed Nov. 2025).

externalOtherAllocations._notes: Facts researched during Step 4 verification pass that could not be confirmed against primary-source text — the City's own web properties (greensboro-nc.gov) returned HTTP 403 on every direct fetch attempted in this session, and the corroborating local-news alternative was rate-limited. Included per the explicit fallback instruction to mark such claims single-source-unconfirmed rather than omit them, since the underlying figures are still plausibly real and specifically relevant (Central Library is a named Table 12 building and the subject of Prioritized Action 15 / EI-G2).

centralLibraryArpaAllocation.name: ARPA allocation for Central Library Roofing and HVAC System/Cooling Tower
centralLibraryArpaAllocation.packageDescription: Reported as covering Central Library Roofing/HVAC ($1,575,000), Greensboro Cultural Center Roof and Various Maintenance/Repairs ($3,499,652), and Parks & Recreation Aquatics Improvements ($1,100,000) — approx. 42% of the City's $59.4M total ARPA award.
centralLibraryArpaAllocation.possibleRelevance: If accurate, this would be the funding source behind the Central Library roof replacement referenced in the 2025 Progress Report under EI-G2 / Prioritized Action 15 — which the Progress Report itself describes only as 'OSR is identifying funding' for remaining corrective measures, without naming ARPA. Not asserted as confirmed; flagged as a lead worth a human follow-up, not encoded as fact anywhere else in this dataset.
centralLibraryArpaAllocation.verificationAttempts: Three City of Greensboro news-release URLs referencing this allocation (News/17559, News/17289, News/17248) all returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch. A Yes! Weekly article covering the same Council vote returned HTTP 429 (rate limited) on the one retry attempted. No primary-source text was successfully retrieved in this research pass — figures are drawn entirely from search-engine result summaries.

externalGrants._notes: Independently verified via web research during Step 3 (not present in either SEP PDF). All five fund the Greensboro Transit Agency (GTA) electric/hybrid bus fleet and are linked to departments.json > transportation-diesel (TR-D-G1). Confidence varies per entry — the first four were confirmed against full primary-source text; the fifth (FY23 grant) is sourced to search-result summaries of a paywalled article and is flagged lower-confidence accordingly.

grants.name: FTA Low-No Emission Grant (GTA zero-emission fleet expansion)
grants.purpose: 6 battery-electric buses, 12 hybrid-diesel buses, and 26 chargers for the Greensboro Transit Agency.
grants.significance: The strongest documented evidence in this entire dataset that the 2022 Strategic Energy Plan directly caused a funded outcome — see quote below.

quote.speaker: Reginald Mason, Greensboro Transit Director
quote.text: This grant will help GTA's transition to a zero-emission fleet in support of the City's Strategic Energy Plan.

citation.publisher: Gillig (republishing City of Greensboro Transit Department announcement)
citation.title: Transit Department Awarded $22.4 Million for Battery-Electric Buses, Hybrid Buses and Vehicle Chargers

grants.confidence: high — fetched and confirmed from full primary source text; also appears in FTA's own FY24 Bus and Low- and No-Emission Grant Awards list and City of Greensboro news coverage.

citation.title: Transit Department Awarded $22.4 Million for Battery-Electric Buses, Hybrid Buses and Vehicle Chargers

grants.source: Federal Transit Administration (Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funding)

externallyVerifiedRenewableAssets._notes: Real, City-related renewable energy assets with no mention in either SEP document and no home in the SEP's department-based goal structure. Independently verified via Legistar (primary source, fetched directly) and web search (secondary, for as-built operational details — the City's own 'Solar Energy Central' page could not be fetched directly, HTTP 403, so as-built figures below carry a lower confidence rating than the Legistar-sourced authorization terms).

authorizingResolution.fullTitle: Resolution Authorizing an Amendment to the White Street Landfill Gas Utilization Project Agreement with Petra Engineering to Include a Solar Facility at the White Street Landfill
authorizingResolution.leasePaymentDestination: Solid Waste Capital Reserve & Operating Fund
authorizingResolution.financingStructureNote: This is NOT the textbook Solar Power Purchase Agreement (SPPA) described in the SEP (p.59), where 'the City provides the site... and purchases the generated electricity from the solar project at a low-cost.' Under the actual agreement, the developer sells the generated electricity to Duke Energy directly — the City receives land-lease rent (~$6,000/year), not discounted power — with a City option to purchase the solar equipment outright after year 7. It achieves a similar zero-capital-outlay renewable-siting outcome to an SPPA, but is structurally a land-lease / independent power producer arrangement, not an SPPA. This corrects an initial mischaracterization as 'the first concrete instance of the SPPA mechanism.'
authorizingResolution.confidence: high — fetched and confirmed from the full primary Legistar record.

citation.title: Resolution Authorizing an Amendment to the White Street Landfill Gas Utilization Project Agreement with Petra Engineering to Include a Solar Facility
citation.note: Not referenced in either SEP source document — found via independent research.

asBuilt.acreageDiscrepancyNote: As-built acreage (~30 acres, per aggregated secondary sources) differs from the 15-20 acres in the 2015 authorizing resolution — plausibly revised upward before construction, but not confirmed against a primary document in this research pass.
asBuilt.operatorNameNote: The 2015 authorizing resolution names the counterparty as 'Petra Engineering'; current sources use 'ReNew Petra.' Plausibly a rebrand of the same company, not independently confirmed.
asBuilt.homesEquivalentDiscrepancyNote: One aggregated secondary source states 650 homes-equivalent rather than 65 — an order-of-magnitude discrepancy not resolved in this research pass. 65 is used here as it matches the more specific source sentence found ('became operational in October 2020 and provides enough power to supply 65 homes'), but this should be spot-checked against the City's own Solar Energy Central page (fetch blocked, HTTP 403, during this pass) before being treated as final.
asBuilt.confidence: medium — drawn from search-result aggregation, not a single fetched primary source. The City's own 'Solar Energy Central' page (https://www.greensboro-nc.gov/departments/office-of-sustainability-and-resilience/leed-for-cities/solar-energy-central) is very likely the origin of these figures but returned HTTP 403 when fetched directly in this research pass — worth a manual re-check.

otherFinancingMechanismsIdentifiedInSep2.note: These are financing strategies described in the 2022 SEP (p.58-61), not confirmed active funding as of the 2025 report unless noted above.

mechanisms.name: Energy Savings Performance Contracting (ESPC)
mechanisms.note: City has previously executed two performance contracts for building system improvements.

mechanisms.name: Green Revolving Fund
mechanisms.note: Proposed; reuses energy-project savings to fund further projects.

mechanisms.name: Green Fee
mechanisms.note: Proposed small surcharge on City services, deposited into a Green Revolving Fund.

mechanisms.name: Energy or Green Bonds
mechanisms.note: Special General Obligation Bonds proposed for large multi-year energy projects.

mechanisms.name: American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) funds
mechanisms.note: City allocated $59 million from ARPA (2021); a portion identified as usable for water/sewer infrastructure improvements relevant to SEP cost-burden goals.

mechanisms.name: Solar Power Purchase Agreement (SPPA)
mechanisms.note: Third party owns/operates/maintains solar; City purchases generated electricity at low cost. The White Street Landfill solar facility (see externallyVerifiedRenewableAssets above) is a related-but-distinct land-lease/independent-power-producer arrangement — not a true instance of this mechanism, since the City doesn't purchase the power. See that entry's financingStructureNote for the distinction.

mechanisms.name: Duke Energy Solar Rebate
mechanisms.note: $0.75/watt incentive, $75,000 max, 100 kW system max, offered yearly until claimed.

mechanisms.name: North Carolina Clean Energy Fund (NCCEF)
mechanisms.note: Newly formed (as of 2022) Green Bank investing in efficiency/renewable projects benefiting underserved populations.

mechanisms.name: Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs)
mechanisms.note: Purchasable via Duke Energy's Green Source Advantage program.

mechanisms.name: Carbon offsets, incl. bio-char production
mechanisms.note: Field Operations proposed an Air Curtain Burner to convert yard waste into carbon-negative bio-char instead of mulch/compost.

contracts.name: Energy management & data analysis platform
contracts.amountNote: Contract value not disclosed in source.
contracts.purpose: Comprehensive energy management and data analysis platform; identifies energy-efficiency measures, enables reductions in energy use, helps identify optimal Duke Energy rates for ~1,100 City electrical service accounts.
contracts.status: Development essentially complete; interface fine-tuning/customization in progress.

contracts.name: Updated GHG inventory (2022-2024)
contracts.amountNote: Contract value not disclosed in source.
contracts.purpose: Prepare updated GHG inventories for 2022, 2023, and 2024 (community-wide and municipal operations), supporting LEED for Cities recertification.

contracts.name: Tier 1 building energy audits (Level 1 / Level 2)
contracts.contractAwardedNote: Exact award date not stated in source; audits described as completed during 2025.
contracts.amountNote: Contract value not disclosed in source.
contracts.purpose: Audit the City's 10-highest energy-using buildings (incl. Central Library) and recommend energy conservation measures. Level 1 audits identified needed maintenance/corrective measures; SCI suggested more-detailed Level 2 audits, which OSR is working to fund.
contracts.status: Level 1 audits complete; Level 2 audits and funding for identified measures pending.

findings.note: Abandoned accounts are utility accounts at locations the City no longer owns, or where the utility service is no longer necessary.
findings.recoveryMethod: Disputing incorrect billing amounts and closely monitoring new service requests.
